results.possibleIdentity: Bettina Brendel (1922–2009) was a German-American abstract painter, printmaker, and lecturer known for her lifelong investigation into the intersection of art and science. Born in Lüneburg, Germany, she immigrated to the United States in 1951 and eventually settled in Los Angeles. Her work is characterized by its exploration of physical phenomena, including quantum mechanics, subatomic particles, light, and energy. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, she was particularly active in bridging these disciplines; notably, she attended the New School for Social Research in New York from 1968 to 1969 to study the history and theory of physics. In 1972, she met with Nobel Prize-winning physicist Werner Heisenberg, whose theories on uncertainty and subatomic particles deeply influenced her artistic practice. Brendel is associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement and was featured in major retrospectives concerning the synergy between technology and art, such as the Getty’s 'PST ART: Art & Science Collide' and the exhibition 'Particles and Waves: Southern California Abstraction and Science, 1945–1990' at the Palm Springs Art Museum. Her significant works include 'Particles or Waves?' (1969), 'A Numbered Universe,' and 'Computer Joy,' the latter of which incorporated binary code and pixelated patterns to humanize technological language.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Richter (1929–2025) was a prominent American documentary filmmaker and producer based in New York City, active from the late 1950s through the early 2020s. He is best known for founding Richter Productions in 1968 after a career as a producer for the Edward R. Murrow and Fred Friendly 'CBS Reports' unit. His work frequently explored the intersection of technology, science, and social justice, which aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Notably, he produced 'The Age of Intelligent Machines' (1987), a documentary exploring artificial intelligence in collaboration with Ray Kurzweil. His films from the late 1960s and 1970s include 'HHH: What Manner of Man' (1968) and the Oscar-nominated environmental documentary 'The Gifts' (1970). Richter was a central figure in the New York independent media scene, serving as the volunteer President of the Association of Independent Video and Filmmakers (AIVF) for ten years and being a long-standing member of the New Day Films collective. His work is also preserved in the Political Art Documentation/Distribution (PAD/D) archive at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), which documents socially responsive art projects in New York.

Robert Richter is a contemporary visual artist born in Milwaukee who became active in New York City during the 1960s and 1970s. During this period, he worked across various disciplines, including painting stage sets, mural commissions, storefront windows, and even textiles like T-shirts and curtains. His signature style involves unique oil paintings on wood with integral, hand-carved frames that he creates himself. His work from the 1970s includes modern figurative and landscape paintings such as 'Sunset' and 'Green Apple.' While primarily known for his traditional and 'outsider' landscape and still-life works, his presence in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and his varied technical output (lithographs from aluminum plates, set design) place him in the same era and location as the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement.

results.possibleIdentity: Daniel Weinberger (also referred to as Daniël Weinberger or Daniel von Weinberger) is a Belgian artist and jeweler who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is recognized for his contributions to the avant-garde art scene, particularly in Antwerp at the International Cultural Centre (ICC). His work spanned performance art, video art, and jewelry. Notable performances included a 1977 bodybuilding act where he "lifted" his own image on television monitors, and he participated in the 1976 film 'Lysistrata' directed by Ludo Mich. As a jeweler, he is featured in the historical survey 'Simply Brilliant: Artist Jewelers of the 1960s and 1970s,' highlighting his position as a maker who considered himself an artist first and a jeweler second during that experimental era.

David Weinberger (born 1950) is a philosopher and technologist who, while primarily known for his later work on the impact of the internet on knowledge (e.g., 'Small Pieces Loosely Joined' and 'Everything is Miscellaneous'), was active in the mid-1970s as a creative writer. In 1976, at the age of 26, he began writing jokes for the 'Inside Woody Allen' comic strip. In contemporary scholarship, his theories on distributed authorship and collaborative systems are frequently cited to analyze the legacy of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted similar concepts of shared creative authority between artists and engineers in the 1960s and 1970s.

Peter J. Weinberger is a prominent computer scientist and engineer who joined Bell Laboratories in 1970. While not primarily known as an artist, he was a key figure at Bell Labs during the height of its collaboration with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He is best known as one of the creators of the AWK programming language and was a manager in the Computer Science Research Center. His likeness famously became the subject of an early digital image-editing experiment at Bell Labs in the 1980s, where his face was digitized and stenciled across the lab and nearby landmarks, reflecting the playful intersection of technology and imagery common in the Bell Labs community.

results.possibleIdentity: Mark A. Straus (also known as Mark Straus) is an artist, collector, and physician who was active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is noted in the curatorial records of the Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden (specifically within files from 1971) alongside research into Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and other collaborative art ventures, such as Sandra Smith's project 'Eat - A Venture in Collaborative Art'. Originally beginning his career as a painter, Straus is also widely recognized as the founder of the MARC STRAUS gallery in New York and the Hudson Valley Museum of Contemporary Art (HV MOCA). His early career coincided with the height of the E.A.T. movement's activities, and he was involved with major curators of the era like Cynthia Jaffee McCabe, who researched artistic collaborations and kinetic art during the 1970s.

Philip A. Straus (1916–2004) and his wife Lynn G. Straus were prominent American art collectors and philanthropists active in the 1960s and 1970s. While Philip Straus was primarily an investment banker and Lynn was a teacher, they were deeply involved in the experimental art scene of the time. In 1969, they began building one of the most significant private collections of Edvard Munch's work, emphasizing the artist's technical and experimental processes. They were major donors to the Harvard Art Museums, where the 'Straus Center for Conservation and Technical Studies' is named in their honor. Their interest in the 'technical' and 'experimental' aspects of art production often brought them into the same circles as organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted the collaboration between artists and technical specialists.

results.possibleIdentity: David Puchkoff (often referred to as D. Puchkoff) is an engineer, inventor, and creative professional who has been active in New York City's artistic and technical communities since the late 1960s. He is widely recognized for his innovative architectural and design projects that blend nature with urban living, most notably the creation of a 'rooftop meadow' and a rustic country cottage atop a six-story co-op building in the West Village. This project, which he developed with architect Lawrence Tobe, has been featured in major publications like The New York Times and National Geographic as a model for sustainable urban technology and community-focused design. Puchkoff is also an inventor with several patents, including systems for boat propulsion and maritime safety ('storm havens'). His professional background as an engineer and his long-term presence in the Soho and West Village creative scenes—including his association with firms like Spring Street Associates and D. Puchkoff Associates Ltd—place him within the network of collaborators typically involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization dedicated to fostering partnerships between artists and engineers. Additionally, he is a photographer whose work has documented major social events, a dedicated sailor who skippers the wooden yacht Prima Donna, and a host of musical and artistic gatherings in his unique loft and rooftop space.
